title: Ginger, herb profile
subtitle: Zingiber officinale: the kitchen's most versatile medicinal herb
excerpt: A materia medica profile of ginger root, its traditional actions, therapeutic uses, preparation methods, and safety. The most commercially familiar and evidence-supported herb in the western herbal pharmacopoeia, present in every kitchen.
sourceNotes: Maud Grieve, A Modern Herbal (1931), ginger entry. King's American Dispensatory (Felter and Lloyd, 1898). WHO Monograph on Zingiber officinale (1999). EMA HMPC monograph on Zingiberis rhizoma (2012).

glossaryTerms.definition: The primary pungent compounds in fresh ginger root, responsible for its characteristic heat and anti-nausea and anti-inflammatory properties. Converted to shogaols on drying.

content.text: Zingiber officinale, ginger, is the rhizome of a tropical grass native to South Asia, cultivated commercially across Southeast Asia, India, China, and West Africa. In the British kitchen it appears as fresh root, dried ground spice, crystallised ginger, and extract. In herbal medicine it is used fresh, dried, or as a tincture. Fresh and dried forms are not fully interchangeable: fresh ginger is richer in

, while drying converts some gingerols to shogaols, which are more potent anti-inflammatory and warming compounds. Culinary ginger and medicinal ginger are the same plant, the dose makes the difference.

Antiemetic (anti-nausea), carminative (digestive wind-relieving), rubefacient (warming and circulatory-stimulating), antispasmodic (gut smooth muscle), diaphoretic (promotes perspiration), expectorant (respiratory congestion). Maud Grieve classifies ginger as stimulant, carminative, and rubefacient. King's American Dispensatory emphasises its antiemetic and circulatory properties.

Nausea of all types: travel sickness, morning sickness at low dose (up to 1 g dried equivalent per day in first trimester, WHO and EMA both list this as acceptable), post-operative nausea, post-chemotherapy nausea (as adjunctive support, not replacement). Digestive complaints: bloating, wind, indigestion. Musculoskeletal: the ginger bath and compress for muscle ache and poor peripheral circulation. Respiratory: the warming bath and hot infusion for the cold, shivery first stage of a respiratory infection.

Ginger is among the most clinically studied herbs. Multiple systematic reviews and meta-analyses support its use for nausea, including pregnancy nausea at low dose. The EMA's 2012 monograph confirms traditional use for nausea and for dyspeptic complaints. The evidence for anti-inflammatory and musculoskeletal applications is more preliminary but consistent across in vitro and observational studies.

Hot infusion: 10 g fresh ginger (a 2 cm piece, thinly sliced) per 250 ml water, steeped covered for 10 minutes. This delivers approximately 1 g dried ginger equivalent, the lower end of the therapeutic dose range. Up to three cups daily for acute nausea. Compress: 30 g fresh ginger simmered in 500 ml water for 15 minutes, applied hot to aching muscles. Bath: 100 g fresh ginger simmered in 1 L water, added to a warm bath. Tincture: 1:5 in 40% spirit, 2, 4 ml up to three times daily.

Culinary and low-therapeutic doses (one to two cups of fresh ginger infusion per day) are safe for most adults. Anticoagulants: ginger has antiplatelet activity, discuss regular therapeutic use with prescriber if on warfarin, aspirin, or clopidogrel. Pregnancy: culinary use is safe; up to 1 g dried equivalent per day for morning sickness is widely documented as acceptable; higher doses (more than 2 g dried equivalent daily) are less studied and best discussed with a midwife. Topical: can cause skin redness and irritation on sensitive skin; always test on a small area before a full bath or compress.
